questions.question:
Using binary full adders and other logic gates (if necessary), design an adder for adding 4-bit numbers (including sign) in 2's complement notation.

How many full adders are required?
questions.to_find: Number of full adders required to add two 4-bit 2's complement numbers

understand.plain: In 2's complement representation, both positive and negative numbers are encoded in 4 bits (including the sign bit). To add two such 4-bit numbers, we treat all bits uniformly — including the sign bit — and simply perform binary addition. Overflow is detected separately. The key insight is that 2's complement addition is identical to unsigned binary addition across all bit positions including the MSB.

keywords.explain: A number representation where negative numbers are encoded by inverting all bits and adding 1. The MSB serves as the sign bit but participates in addition like any other bit.
keywords.example: -3 in 4-bit 2's complement is 1101; adding 0011 (3) gives 10000, and ignoring the carry-out yields 0000 = 0, which is correct.

keywords.explain: A combinational circuit that adds three 1-bit inputs (A, B, Carry-in) and produces a 1-bit Sum and a 1-bit Carry-out.
keywords.example: Full adder with A=1, B=1, Cin=0 gives Sum=0, Cout=1.

stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='260' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>4 Full Adders (one per bit, including sign bit)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four full adders connected in a ripple-carry chain. Each FA handles one bit position (bits 0 through 3, where bit 3 is the sign bit). Carry propagates from bit 0 to bit 3. The initial carry-in to FA0 is 0.

given.aim: Count the number of full adders needed to add two 4-bit 2's complement numbers

terms.meaning: A number represented in 4 bits where the MSB is the sign bit but all bits (including sign) participate equally in addition
terms.connects: All 4 bits must be added, so we need a circuit covering all 4 bit positions

terms.meaning: A circuit that adds A_i + B_i + Cin and produces Sum_i and Cout
terms.connects: One FA is needed per bit position

terms.meaning: The initial carry into bit position 0 of the adder chain
terms.example: For pure addition, Cin=0; for subtraction using 2's complement, Cin=1
terms.connects: Since Cin is a constant 0 for addition, a half-adder could handle bit 0, but using FA throughout is the standard design

given.plan: In 2's complement addition, both operands are treated as standard binary numbers across all 4 bit positions. One full adder handles each bit. Since there are 4 bits (b0 through b3, including the sign bit b3), 4 full adders are required, connected in a ripple-carry chain.

steps.title: Understand 2's complement addition
steps.formula_raw: A + B (mod 2^4) in binary, sign bit participates like any other bit
steps.apply: For 4-bit 2's complement, addition is performed identically to unsigned 4-bit binary addition. Each of the 4 bit positions requires an adder. No special logic is needed for the sign bit during addition — overflow is detected afterwards if needed.
steps.note: This is the beauty of 2's complement: the hardware for addition is the same as unsigned addition.

steps.title: Count required full adders
steps.formula_raw: Number of FA = number of bit positions = 4
steps.apply:
Bit 0 (LSB): FA with Cin=0, produces S0 and C1.
Bit 1: FA with C1, produces S1 and C2.
Bit 2: FA with C2, produces S2 and C3.
Bit 3 (MSB/sign): FA with C3, produces S3 and Cout.
Total: 4 full adders.
steps.note: The carry-out from the MSB position is discarded (or used for overflow detection by XOR-ing with the carry into the MSB).

solution.result: 4 full adders are required to add two 4-bit 2's complement numbers.

questions.formula_note: 2's complement addition uses standard binary ripple-carry addition across all bit positions including the sign bit; one full adder per bit.

questions.question:
Fill in the blanks:
In the two-bit full-adder/subtractor unit shown in the figure below, when the switch is in position ___________ the circuit performs addition using ___________ arithmetic.
questions.to_find: Switch position and type of arithmetic used when switch is in that position

understand.plain: A combined adder/subtractor circuit uses a mode control switch. In position 1, it performs unsigned binary addition. In position 2, it feeds the carry-in as 1 and inverts the second operand, which is exactly the operation needed to compute A - B using 2's complement: B_inverted + A + 1 = A + (-B) in 2's complement.

keywords.explain: A circuit that can perform both addition and subtraction. Subtraction A - B is implemented as A + (~B) + 1, where ~B is the bitwise complement of B and +1 is provided via Cin=1.
Subtraction is performed by adding the 2's complement of the subtrahend. This requires inverting the subtrahend bits and adding 1 via the carry-in.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 160' width='400' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='140' y='50' width='120' height='60' class='hi' rx='8'/><text x='200' y='83' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2-bit FA</text><text x='200' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Adder/Subtractor</text><rect x='20' y='30' width='60' height='25' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='50' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Switch</text><line x1='80' y1='42' x2='140' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='95' y='38' class='sub ink'>pos 1: Cin=0</text><text x='90' y='55' class='sub ink'>pos 2: Cin=1</text><text x='10' y='130' class='sub ink'>pos 1: A+B (unsigned)</text><text x='10' y='148' class='sub ink'>pos 2: A-B (2's complement)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 2-bit adder/subtractor unit with a switch that selects between two positions. Position 1 sets Cin=0 for addition; position 2 sets Cin=1 and inverts B inputs for 2's complement subtraction.

given.aim: Identify the switch position for addition and the arithmetic type used

terms.meaning: Connects Cin=0 and passes B directly — performs unsigned addition A + B
terms.connects: This is standard binary addition, not 2's complement

terms.meaning: Connects Cin=1 and inverts B inputs — performs A + (~B) + 1 = A - B in 2's complement
terms.connects: This is 2's complement subtraction, but the question asks which position performs addition using 2's complement — the entire system uses 2's complement number representation

terms.meaning: Number system where negative numbers are represented by bit inversion plus 1; the hardware naturally handles signed addition/subtraction
terms.connects: When the circuit uses 2's complement representation, position 2 enables proper signed addition/subtraction

given.plan: In the adder/subtractor circuit, position 2 sets Cin=1 and XORs B with 1 (inverts B). This gives A + (~B) + 1 = A + (2's complement of B) = A - B. The circuit uses 2's complement arithmetic in this configuration.

steps.title: Analyze switch position 1
steps.apply: When switch is in position 1: B inputs pass through unmodified, Cin = 0. The circuit computes A + B using standard unsigned binary addition.
steps.note: This is straightforward unsigned (sign-magnitude or unsigned) addition.

steps.title: Analyze switch position 2
steps.apply: When switch is in position 2: B inputs are inverted (XOR with 1), and Cin = 1. So the circuit computes A + (~B) + 1. By definition, ~B + 1 = -B in 2's complement. Therefore the result is A + (-B) = A - B using 2's complement arithmetic.
steps.note: The switch position 2 enables 2's complement subtraction. In the context of the fill-in-the-blank, the answer is: switch position 2, using 2's complement arithmetic.

questions.formula_note: 2's complement subtraction: A - B = A + (~B) + 1; implemented by inverting B and setting carry-in = 1.

questions.question:
An N-bit carry lookahead adder, where N is a multiple of 4, employs ICs 74181 (4-bit ALU) and 74182 (4-bit carry lookahead generator). The minimum addition time using the best architecture for this adder is

A. proportional to N
B. proportional to log(N)
C. a constant
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Minimum addition time for an N-bit carry lookahead adder built from 74181 and 74182 ICs

understand.plain: The 74181 is a 4-bit ALU and the 74182 is a carry lookahead unit that eliminates the ripple delay within a 4-bit group. When we chain multiple such groups for an N-bit adder, the inter-group carry can again be made lookahead by using a hierarchical (tree) structure of 74182 units. With a binary tree of lookahead generators, the carry propagation depth is O(log N), making the total addition time proportional to log(N).

keywords.explain: An adder architecture where carry signals are computed in parallel using generate (G) and propagate (P) signals, avoiding the sequential carry ripple.
keywords.example: A 4-bit CLA computes all carries simultaneously using G_i = A_i AND B_i and P_i = A_i XOR B_i.

keywords.explain: An IC that accepts group generate/propagate signals from up to 4 blocks (each block being a 74181) and produces the carry-out for each group simultaneously.
keywords.example: One 74182 handles carries for four 74181 units (16 bits total) in constant time.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 200' width='500' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='190' y='10' width='120' height='40' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='250' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>74182 (L2)</text><rect x='50' y='90' width='100' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='100' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>74182 (L1)</text><rect x='200' y='90' width='100' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='250' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>74182 (L1)</text><rect x='350' y='90' width='100' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='400' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>74182 (L1)</text><rect x='10' y='160' width='60' height='30' style='fill:#e8f5e9;stroke:#2e7d32;stroke-width:1.5;' rx='3'/><text x='40' y='179' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>74181</text><rect x='80' y='160' width='60' height='30' style='fill:#e8f5e9;stroke:#2e7d32;stroke-width:1.5;' rx='3'/><text x='110' y='179' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>74181</text><rect x='180' y='160' width='60' height='30' style='fill:#e8f5e9;stroke:#2e7d32;stroke-width:1.5;' rx='3'/><text x='210' y='179' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>74181</text><rect x='250' y='160' width='60' height='30' style='fill:#e8f5e9;stroke:#2e7d32;stroke-width:1.5;' rx='3'/><text x='280' y='179' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>74181</text><line x1='100' y1='125' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='250' y1='125' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='400' y1='125' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='250' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Tree of 74182s: depth = log_4(N/4), time = O(log N)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hierarchical tree of 74182 carry lookahead generators. Level 1 has multiple 74182 units each driving 4 74181 ALUs. Level 2 has a single 74182 combining the group carries from Level 1. The tree depth is proportional to log(N), giving O(log N) addition time.

given.aim: Determine how minimum addition time scales with N for a hierarchical CLA using 74181 and 74182 ICs

terms.meaning: Performs arithmetic/logic on 4-bit operands; provides group generate G* and propagate P* outputs for carry lookahead
terms.example: Four 74181s cover bits 0-15 of a 16-bit addition
terms.connects: Each 74181 handles 4 bits; N/4 units needed for an N-bit adder

terms.meaning: Accepts G* and P* from up to 4 groups (74181s) and generates carry-out for each group simultaneously in O(1) time
terms.example: One 74182 computes C4, C8, C12, C16 simultaneously from 4 74181 groups
terms.connects: Arranging 74182s in a tree of depth log_4(N/4) = O(log N) gives carry in O(log N) time

terms.meaning: Multiple levels of 74182 units arranged as a tree; higher levels compute inter-group carries, each level adding one gate delay
terms.example: For N=64: 4 74181s per group -> 16 groups covered by 4 74182s at L1 -> 1 74182 at L2; depth = 2 = log_4(16)
terms.connects: Total carry delay proportional to tree height = log(N)

given.plan: Each 74181 produces group G*/P* in constant time. One 74182 resolves carries for 4 groups in constant time. By building a tree of 74182 units, we need log_4(N/4) levels. Since log_4(N/4) = O(log N), the total addition time is proportional to log(N).

steps.title: Determine the number of 74181 groups
steps.apply: Each 74181 handles 4 bits. For an N-bit adder, N/4 groups exist. Each group produces group-generate (G*) and group-propagate (P*) signals in O(1) time.
steps.note: This is the first level: O(1) time to get all group G*/P* signals.

steps.title: Build hierarchical 74182 tree for carries
steps.apply:
One 74182 can handle 4 groups. For N/4 groups at the base level:
- Level 1: ceil((N/4)/4) = N/16 units of 74182
- Level 2: ceil((N/16)/4) = N/64 units
- ...
- The tree has depth log_4(N/4) levels.
Since each level adds a constant gate delay, total carry propagation time = O(log_4(N)) = O(log N).
steps.note: The answer is B: proportional to log(N). This is the key advantage of hierarchical CLA over ripple-carry (O(N)) or flat CLA (limited to small N due to fan-in).

questions.formula_note: Hierarchical carry lookahead using a tree of 74182 generators gives O(log N) carry propagation depth, making total addition time proportional to log(N).

questions.question:
The number of full and half-adders required to add 16-bit numbers is

A. 8 half-adders, 8 full-adders
B. 1 half-adder, 15 full-adders
C. 16 half-adders, 0 full-adders
D. 4 half-adders, 12 full-adders
questions.to_find: Number of half-adders and full-adders needed to add two 16-bit numbers

understand.plain: When adding two 16-bit numbers using a ripple-carry adder, the least significant bit (bit 0) has no carry-in, so it only adds two 1-bit inputs — this can be done with a half-adder. All remaining 15 bit positions (bits 1 through 15) have a carry-in from the previous stage, so they require full-adders (which accept three inputs: A, B, and Cin).

keywords.explain: A circuit that adds two 1-bit inputs (A, B) and produces Sum and Carry-out. It cannot accept a carry-in.
keywords.example: 1 + 1 = Sum=0, Carry=1 (half adder). Used only for the LSB where Cin=0.

keywords.explain: A circuit that adds three 1-bit inputs (A, B, Cin) and produces Sum and Carry-out. Required for all bit positions that receive a carry from the previous stage.
keywords.example: A=1, B=1, Cin=1 -> Sum=1, Cout=1 (full adder handles all combinations).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 120' width='520' height='120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='60' height='45' style='fill:#CECBF6;stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:3;' rx='5'/><text x='40' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>HA</text><text x='40' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>bit 0</text><rect x='100' y='20' width='60' height='45' class='bg' rx='5'/><text x='130' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>FA</text><text x='130' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>bit 1</text><rect x='180' y='20' width='60' height='45' class='bg' rx='5'/><text x='210' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>FA</text><text x='210' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>bit 2</text><text x='290' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>...</text><rect x='360' y='20' width='60' height='45' class='bg' rx='5'/><text x='390' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>FA</text><text x='390' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>bit 15</text><line x1='70' y1='42' x2='100' y2='42' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='42' x2='180' y2='42' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='240' y1='42' x2='265' y2='42' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='325' y1='42' x2='360' y2='42' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='420' y1='42' x2='450' y2='42' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='455' y='38' class='sub mut'>Cout</text><text x='260' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>1 HA (bit 0) + 15 FA (bits 1-15) = 16 total</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 16-bit ripple-carry adder chain. Bit 0 uses a half-adder (no carry-in). Bits 1 through 15 each use a full-adder (carry-in from previous stage). Total: 1 half-adder and 15 full-adders.

given.aim: Find the count of half-adders and full-adders required for a 16-bit ripple-carry adder

terms.meaning: Adding two 16-bit binary numbers bit by bit from LSB to MSB, propagating carry
terms.example: Adding 0xABCD + 0x1234 requires handling all 16 bit positions
terms.connects: Determines the number of adder stages needed

terms.meaning: The least significant bit position; has no carry-in (Cin = 0 always)
terms.example: Adding A0 + B0 with no Cin needs only a half-adder
terms.connects: Only one half-adder is needed — for bit 0

terms.meaning: All bit positions except the LSB; each receives a carry from the previous stage
terms.example: Bit 1 gets carry-out from the half-adder at bit 0
terms.connects: Each of these 15 positions requires a full-adder

given.plan: Bit 0 has no carry-in, so a half-adder suffices. Bits 1-15 each have a carry-in from the previous stage, so full-adders are needed. Total: 1 half-adder + 15 full-adders.

steps.title: Analyze bit 0 (LSB)
steps.formula_raw: Bit 0: Sum = A0 XOR B0, Carry = A0 AND B0
steps.apply: The LSB has no carry coming in (Cin = 0 since this is the first bit). A half-adder with two inputs (A0, B0) is sufficient to produce Sum0 and Carry1.

steps.title: Analyze bits 1 through 15
steps.formula_raw: Bits 1-15: Sum_i = A_i XOR B_i XOR C_i, Carry_{i+1} = majority(A_i, B_i, C_i)
steps.apply: Each of bits 1, 2, ..., 15 receives a carry-in from the previous stage. Therefore each requires a full-adder. That is 15 full-adders (for bit positions 1 through 15).
steps.note: Full-adder count = 15. Total adders = 1 HA + 15 FA = 16, matching the 16-bit word length.

questions.formula_note: For an N-bit ripple-carry adder: 1 half-adder for the LSB (no carry-in) and N-1 full-adders for the remaining bits.
